name: Dizang
description: Dizang, also known as Ksitigarbha in Sanskrit, is a revered bodhisattva in East Asian Buddhism, particularly within Chinese, Korean, Japanese, and Vietnamese traditions. He is widely recognized as the bodhisattva who made a great vow to take responsibility for the instruction of all beings in the six worlds between the death of Gautama Buddha and the rise of Maitreya, as well as not to achieve Buddhahood until all hells are emptied. He is often depicted as a monk, typically with a shaven head, wearing simple robes, and carrying a staff (khakkhara) to force open the gates of hell and a wish-fulfilling jewel (cintamani) to light up the darkness. His compassionate nature and dedication to liberating beings from suffering, especially those in the lower realms of existence, have made him a central figure in Pure Land Buddhism and a powerful symbol of salvation and hope. Devotion to Dizang is believed to alleviate suffering, reduce negative karma, and ultimately lead to enlightenment.
subtitle: Bodhisattva of the Great Vow

keyMyths.title: Dizang's Vow
keyMyths.description: The most prominent myth surrounding Dizang is his vow to postpone his own Buddhahood until all hells are emptied and all beings are liberated from suffering. This unwavering commitment underscores his boundless compassion and dedication to the salvation of all sentient beings. This vow is central to his iconography and the devotion he inspires.
keyMyths.source: Buddhist Sutras, particularly the Ksitigarbha Bodhisattva Purva Pranidhana Sutra

keyMyths.title: Saving His Mother
keyMyths.description: Dizang's past life as a Brahmin maiden is often recounted. In this life, his mother committed evil deeds and was reborn in the hell realms. Distraught, the maiden sold all her possessions and used the proceeds to make offerings to the Buddha, praying for her mother's release. Her heartfelt devotion and merit allowed her to descend into hell and ultimately liberate her mother, demonstrating the power of filial piety and the efficacy of Dizang's compassion.

keyMyths.title: The Ten Kings of Hell
keyMyths.description: Dizang is closely associated with the Ten Kings of Hell in Chinese Buddhism. He acts as an advocate for the deceased, helping them navigate the trials and tribulations of the afterlife and offering them opportunities to reduce their karmic debt. This role reinforces his image as a compassionate guide and protector in the face of death and judgment.
keyMyths.source: Popular Chinese Buddhist beliefs and iconography

keyMyths.title: Manifestations and Miracles
keyMyths.description: Numerous stories recount Dizang's miraculous interventions in the lives of devotees. These tales often involve him appearing in dreams, visions, or even physical form to offer guidance, protection, and relief from suffering. These narratives solidify his reputation as a powerful and accessible bodhisattva who actively responds to the prayers and needs of those who seek his help.
keyMyths.source: Collected accounts of miracles and experiences of devotees

sources.author: Jan Yun-hua
sources.work: A Chronicle of Buddhism in China, 581-960 A.D.
sources.description: Provides historical context for the development of Dizang's cult in China.
sources.text: Provides historical context for the development of Dizang's cult in China.

sources.author: Shinohara, Koichi
sources.work: Two Sources of Chinese Buddhist Biographies: Stupa Inscriptions and Miracle Stories
sources.description: Examines the role of miracle stories in shaping the image of Dizang and other Buddhist figures.
sources.text: Examines the role of miracle stories in shaping the image of Dizang and other Buddhist figures.

sources.author: Teiser, Stephen F.
sources.work: The Scripture on the Ten Kings
sources.description: Discusses the relationship between Dizang and the Ten Kings of Hell in Chinese Buddhism.
sources.text: Discusses the relationship between Dizang and the Ten Kings of Hell in Chinese Buddhism.

sources.author: Brook, Timothy
sources.work: Praying for Power: Buddhism and the Formation of Gentry Society in Late-Ming China
sources.description: Explores the social and political context of Buddhist practices, including devotion to Dizang.
sources.text: Explores the social and political context of Buddhist practices, including devotion to Dizang.

sources.author: Getty, Alice
sources.work: The Gods of Northern Buddhism: Their History, Iconography, and Progressive Evolution Through the Northern Buddhist Countries
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of Buddhist deities, including Dizang/Ksitigarbha, and their iconography.
